data.wave_or_subset: Single wave during Italian nationwide lockdown, March 18–30, 2020; stimuli included NonCovid Verbal, Covid Verbal, Covid Meme, and Covid Strip items
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual humor-item rating provided by a participant
data.notes: Participants were located across Italy with geography captured at the province level; ratings collected on 7-point scales for funniness and aversiveness; personality (CHS) and optimism (LOTR) measured; psychological distance measures included perceived infection risk, spatial distance from Codogno, and social distance (known cases). Summary reports conflicting participant counts (1,751 vs. 1,903). Mixed-effects models included crossed random effects for participant, item, and province.

method.description: The study deployed an online survey during the first Italian lockdown to measure perceived funniness and aversiveness of humor items related to Covid-19 (verbal jokes, memes, and strips) and non-pandemic verbal jokes. It collected demographics, Coping Humor Scale (CHS), Life Orientation Test–Revised (LOTR), and psychological distance indicators (perceived risk, spatial distance from the outbreak epicenter, and social distance). Linear mixed-effects models in R (lme4) with crossed random effects (participants, items, province) assessed how humor type, personality, and distance predicted funniness and aversiveness.
steps: Design an online survey including humor stimuli across four categories: NonCovid Verbal, Covid Verbal, Covid Meme, and Covid Strip.
Collect demographics and administer CHS and LOTR questionnaires.
Measure psychological distance: perceived infection risk, spatial distance from Codogno (epicenter), and social distance (number of known Covid-19 cases among friends/relatives).
Field the survey to participants in Italy during March 18–30, 2020.
Have participants rate each humor item on funniness and aversiveness using 7-point scales.
Geolocate participants by province of completion.
Fit linear mixed-effects models (R, lme4) with crossed random effects for participants, items, and province.
Incrementally add fixed effects (demographics, personality, psychological distance) and compare model fit; estimate effects of humor type, CHS, LOTR, and distance on funniness and aversiveness.
method.models: Linear mixed-effects models (lme4) with crossed random effects for participant, item, and province; hierarchical model comparison via likelihood ratio tests
method.outcome_variable: Funniness rating; Aversiveness rating (both on 7-point scales)
method.independent_variables: Humor type (NonCovid Verbal, Covid Verbal, Covid Meme, Covid Strip); Coping Humor Scale (CHS); Life Orientation Test–Revised (LOTR); perceived infection risk; spatial distance from Codogno; social distance (known Covid-19 cases among acquaintances); demographics (e.g., age, gender, education, region)
method.control_variables: Demographics (age, gender, education, geographic region/province); item identity; participant identity; province of completion (as random effects)

results.summary: Covid-19 related humor was generally more aversive than non-pandemic humor. Memes were the funniest Covid-related format, while strips were less funny and more aversive, particularly when addressing serious themes. Individuals higher in coping humor (CHS) rated jokes as funnier and less aversive; dispositional optimism (LOTR) positively related to funniness for memes. Greater perceived infection risk increased aversiveness, while greater spatial distance from the outbreak epicenter was associated with increased funniness after an initial decrease. Findings underscore heterogeneity in humor appreciation and the roles of personality and psychological distance.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Funniness: Covid Meme vs Covid Verbal
Funniness: Covid Strip vs Covid Verbal
Aversiveness: Covid Verbal vs NonCovid Verbal (difference)
Aversiveness: Covid Strip vs Covid Verbal (difference)
Aversiveness: Perceived infection risk effect (Covid Strip)
